text: So yeah, while Matt and Rowan are setting up here, I just want to introduce them.
Because it's not every day that you get to see people visiting from the future, but you're
about to see that happen right now.
We're very fortunate to be visited by some researchers who are coming to tell us about
the future of science publishing and communication from the future.
It's a special day today.
Rowan is the CEO and founder of Curvenote, where he leads the development of tools designed
to free scientific communication from PDFs.
Rowan is also the founder of the Continuous Science Foundation, a non-profit organization
for the next generation of science communication.
CSF is developing standards to support a more continuous and composable model of science.
Rowan is also visiting his alma mater today.
He has a PhD from UBC in geophysics, computational geophysics, and has received multiple awards
for innovative research dissemination and open education resources, including visible
geology, and interactive geoscience modeling application used by over 1 million students globally.
Matt Akamatsu is an assistant professor of biology at the University of Washington in Seattle.
He's also the co-founder of the Discourse Graphs Project, supported by the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative
and Navigation Fund.
They're creating systems and software tools for modular research in the age of AI.
Matt Akamatsu is a recipient of the 2020 Porter Prize for Research Excellence from the American
Society of Cell Biology, the K99 Pathway to Independence Award, and the Experimental Foundations
Beyond the Journal Award.
Matt Akamatsu received his PhD in biophysics at Yale, did postdoc at Berkeley, and started
at ISLAB in UW.
Take it away, guys.
Matt Akamatsu: Excellent.
Thank you so much.
I'm absolutely thrilled and excited to talk to you today about the movement towards modular
open science and some of the work that we're doing at Continuous Science Foundation and Curb
Note towards these goals.
And so today, science is trapped in this sort of paper-shaped box.
We're communicating our discoveries using print-era concepts that constrain the way that we communicate,
evaluate, and ultimately build towards scientific knowledge and progress.
But the science that's being produced has changed so massively with larger data sets, larger scale
collaborations, continuous sharing of computational notebooks and graphs.
The way that we communicate science has really not kept up to that change.
Our mission at the Continuous Science Foundation is to help move the open science movement from
sort of the sole focus on reading and access, the open access movement, to a future that's more
focused on utility and reuse that's built on these same foundations of openness and designed for
modern discovery patterns, where the code, data, protocols, methods are available.
They're interconnected.
They're modular.
They can be composed together through remixing and sort of importing somebody else's work.
And that really is the ethos of science, is building upon other people's work, is standing on the shoulders of giants.
There's an illusion published by Louis Albert Necker in 1832.
He was a crystallographer.
And this is the Necker cube.
And it's 12 lines on a page.
But if you stare at the bottom, the left sort of side pops out and it changes and it flips orientation.
If you look at the top, again, there's this perceptual reversal.
It has the same underlying data, but different interpretations, different stories that you can tell about
fitting those data points together.
And this is the way that I like to think about modular science, is we have these Lego bricks, these modules of science,
the components, the figures, the equations, the data sets, the scripts, the visualization routines,
those protocols, questions, claims, evidence, and they're in these different spaces.
They can live on the floor, scattered.
They can be sorted into buckets or assembled on a shelf as sort of a published paper.
And so the assembly into a paper, it tells a story.
It's that narrative that introduces a new concept.
It changes minds.
The components of scientific discourse are bundled together into that sort of narrative,
that traditional way of publishing.
This composed artifact, that paper, that story.
But our scientific ways, our sharing industry, and the standards today, they demand that you sort of
glue that trophy together when you share it.
That eight page paper with its four sort of paneled figures, a zip file supplement.
It's all fused together.
And if you want to reuse a figure or a data set, you have to sort of chisel out that figure.
You have to take a screenshot of it, copy that data set.
And as soon as you take it out of that piece of paper, that figure gets amnesia.
It sort of forgets all about its attribution, its trust, its licensing.
It loses its context.
It loses like that license and that credit behind it.
All of that rich metadata that sort of our scientific commons are designed to support.
And so these were really the challenges that prompted the first wave of modular science.
Many people recognize that it's not enough to share an advertisement of your experiment.
You should share the data and the methods and the code underlying it.
And so the prescription in 2010 was for modular science.
It was pretty simple.
You just break it into pieces and you put it into buckets.
And you put it in the appropriate repository for that component.
So protocols.io, you share your protocols.
Figures go to fig share.
Software heritage is the place that you share your code.
A specialized data repository for genomics or microscopy data.
And Zenodo can sort of take up all of the rest.
And so these are the buckets of modular science.
The digital repositories for archiving, tagging, organizing your data, protocols, code, and figures.
And they really are actually excellent buckets.
They exist fit for purpose for science.
But they're largely designed for after the work is done.
When you're starting to organize it, package it up, and put it in these places.
So tidying up your workspace, putting the blocks away, and sort of pointing them from your glued together trophy on a shelf.
And they're structured.
They're organized.
They strive to be these sort of tidy, curated spaces.
And you fill out your intake form, specify your authors, license, description, your ORCID, your ROAR, your funders.
All of these references.
And it's really a very high bar to share work.
And if you have some visualization code or a scratch script that you really know is the secret of how your work hangs together,
it's like the bar for sharing that is so high.
So all of those small things sort of get lost.
They don't fit together on the shelf with that glued together trophy.
And they don't really amount to enough to justify the investments to sort of formally request bucket status.
And that's the aggregation of all of these small components of sort of the workflows and scripts of science fitting together.
That's where the tacit knowledge of science lives of all of those workflow scripts of saying,
yeah, I've seen that before.
I'll grab you the notebook or I'll grab you that script or I've seen that last year and that's a dead end.
And so these buckets are sort of both that very high bar and they're not fully aligned with science and with research,
which is often much more much less structured than sort of the scientific method like laid out on a piece of paper step by step.
That's that's how it works.
It's much more fluid, much more serendipitous.
You need to access all of the pieces to build new things and you sort of dump the buckets out onto the floor.
And this this is the space to build, to experiment, to support that sort of serendipity of science.
Maybe the equivalent of sort of leaving of that Petri dish out and Fleming discovering penicillin in 1928.
Or maybe you have like slightly better lab hygiene protocols and setups and software
so that you don't step on the pieces of Lego on the floor,
but sort of have that goal to access those pieces readily and build them and put them together
so that you can pull data from someone's lab in Tokyo, pull a protocol from Germany and remix them with your ideas
to build something new, to learn something new, to discover something new.
And so these these are the the three states of scientific components and modularity.
They're on the floor, that chaos, that serendipity, that remixing in the buckets organized by types,
protocols, data, repositories, that's that first wave of modular science that was really tackling that problem.
Or they're on the shelf, that composed artifact, that paper, that story.
And all of these are are right. They're they're correct.
They're like good ways that are fit for purpose for that task.
And they're sort of that different side of that necracube, that that illusion that flips and pops in and out for
that right time that you need it for teaching or learning or discovering or building.
And so I think this this is what the next wave of modular science is about, is
not just sort of breaking it apart to share, which is sort of somewhat unnatural, especially if you have
these sort of smaller artifacts that don't fit on a shelf sort of behind that trophy, is thinking
about those sort of aggregate components that that are the connective space of science. And sharing those,
there's such a high bar for for sharing those in in this sort of first wave. And it ends in this sort of
disconnection, this constellation of research artifacts, where you have to tell a fable to imagine how it's all
connected together, instead of actually connecting it together. And the second wave of modular science is
about improving that packaging experience, working with the researcher as they're on the floor, as they're
building, that is where the modularity emerges. You don't write a manuscript top to bottom 10,000 unbroken
words, one after another, with perfect figures, you iterate on the figures, you iterate one panel at a
time, one script at a time, asking for feedback in this sort of modular composable way with with
collaborators, and building up from that modular work. And it's a connected graph of discourse or
computational narratives, supported by modern tooling like Obsidian or Mist Markdown, that can define the
context of that data and how it travels together. And the result of all of this is sort of this
multiplicative additive compounding effect of how these things come together.
And so I think it's also not worth losing sight of the end goal of this is actually to tell some of
these new stories. These compositions are the point of science. And maybe we should aspirationally have
these less be less like trophies on a shelf behind glass, but shared a little bit more continuously a little
bit earlier. And here there's like this meta study that is crying out from here of how teapots are associated
with the Lego bricks on the shelf that you actually want to pull these out and put them together and try
something new. And so that that is like a very different thought of modular science than this first wave. So
instead of thinking about the figures off on their own, we're thinking about the structure of these
artifacts inside. So not import figure, but import figure from a paper. And that can compose all the
way down so that you can get inside of these artifacts and bring modularity with you as it's traveling throughout the ecosystem.
And so I, I just want to give a flavor of sort of the syntax of how we're thinking about,
about modular science in this second wave. And it really does, I think the Lego metaphor works well
here because it does have these specific ways that science fits together. This is the schemas and the
standards and the pieces behind research of how they click together that you can make very different
things. And there isn't glue here. You're also able to take them apart and put them into different compositions.
And that is the goal of the project out of the Continuous Science Foundation called Open Exchange
Architecture, OXA or OXA. They are new standards for modular and composable scientific content. And you can learn
more about that at OXA.dev. And this is a large project that's bringing together a lot of existing
players in the scientific ecosystem, all the way from authoring tools like Korto or Mist Markdown that
people might have started to use to publishers like eLife for traditional manuscripts or Open Archive for
preprints, as well as licensing organizations like Creative Commons who are involved in sort of bringing
all of these together into a modern computational, complete modular and composable format that can
actually support our scientific narratives. And so some of the principles that we have for OXA is,
of course, it's open. It's a JSON schema-based representation. It's a tree-based representation of
the scientific content specifically. And so this is anything from the paragraph, citations, figures,
equations, or code. It is extensible to these sort of new types of data, composable so that you can
bring one person's work and compose it inside of yours, typed down, very similar to the sort of the
at proto schemas, and thinking about that interoperability modularity. And throughout all of this,
really thinking about the computation as a first class citizen within this schema itself.
It's also going through, we're thinking at this stage a lot about the governance and how we're
actually building this up. And so there's an RFC process that actually goes through and we're
beginning this movement, I think, in the right way from that perspective, so that we're actually building
this up and being fit for purpose all the way through. And it also, of course, has an at proto
recognition on that. So you can transfer out of this much more schema, sorry, tree-based schema,
which is sort of the native representation of OXA, to something that's a little closer to
what at proto works with, which is a facet-based piece where you can actually have some of those
annotations, which might be a citation or an additional abbreviation or something that is deeply nested
inside of this schema, but can be distributed from sort of a social perspective. And so there's a lot of
thought going in here. And this is definitely early stages. And so we're inviting a lot of people to come,
be involved in sort of the definition of the schema as it starts to exist. And I want to just give a couple
different flavors with my Curvenote hat on about some of the case studies that we're bringing to bear
with these ideas. And so this Elemental Microscopy is a journal that we're working with that's trying to
do something a little bit more exciting than just sort of a picture on a page with a blowout of
that sort of one and a half terabytes of data is you should actually be able, similar to Google Maps,
to just like zoom into it directly as you're reading, is we have all of these sorts of visuals out there
already. Scientists are using them in their day-to-day work. So we should be able to compose those into the
ways that we are sharing science. And this is speaking to that modularity as well. That one and a half terabytes
doesn't live inside of the paper. It does live in its appropriate bucket, but it can be composed into
the way that we share and read and communicate science. So thinking about not a constellation of
research objects that we're sort of painting the stars, but actually showing you how it all fits together.
The other project that's one of the other projects that Curvenote is working on is a partnership with
Open Archive. Open Archive is the parent organization around Bio Archive and Med Archive, which are the
largest biomedical preprint servers. And we are currently reprocessing 8.1 terabytes of articles to this new
format. And so this is something that's ongoing right now. There's about half a million preprints that are coming together.
And this is going to support
more
exciting reading experiences where you can hover over a figure and bring that in. You can dive into that right away.
And so this is within the context of a single article that you can bring that modular piece to bear
in the context and bring that sort of forward in your way of working. But that also should be able to exist
across research articles. And so some research articles, if you hover over them, are closed access
and they're not that exciting. But if they're open access or a preprint, you should be able to bring
the figure from that to bear right as you're reading. And so really thinking again about this sort of
modular composable way of working that really can improve both the reading experience of a researcher.
Like if if you have a reference that says see figure three in someone at all, like you have to scroll to
the bottom of the page, download the PDF, jump through a paywall, bring it up in your reference manager,
scroll to page 50 and actually get the the figure there. And by the time you've got there, you've lost
all of your context and machines often can't follow all of that work either. And so this,
this I think is thinking about that sort of next wave of modular science, both from the user experience
standpoint, the schemas that are supporting it, as well as the ways that sort of the community can get
involved throughout. And so not thinking about trophies on a shelf that are glued together,
but pieces that you can take. And those are a starting point for the next analysis, lowering the
barrier to entry for inclusion in these buckets that you can maybe package these up with the pieces that
you are sharing and still be indexed in, in these different ways, as well as supporting researchers
as they're working on the floor with better tooling, better graph based waves of working better
computational notebooks that actually support the organization and communication of all of these
results. And so that is, I think the way this new wave of modular science is not saying one of these ways of
working is better than the other, but it's flipping that perspective back and forth between all of these
different ways of working all at once. And so I think the other really exciting piece that was talked
about at the start as well is this is our time. This is our semblance of this space and there is so much
going on that we can actually affect massive change in the way that science is done. So I am Groen Cockett.
I'm the CEO of Curvenote and also one of the co-founders of Continuous Science Foundation and follow me on
Blue Sky. So thank you very much.
- Thank you very much.
- Thank you.
- I think we're doing a few times before.
- Thank you.
- Thank you.
- Thank you.
- Hello, yes, great.
- Hello, yes.
- Great.
- Well, thank you, Rowan, for such an inspiring talk. Rowan and I first met about this time last year
at a Continuous Science Foundation workshop that Rowan organized in Banff in which we converged at
this idea of modular research as a new paradigm for open science. And so I'll show today how our
research lab at the University of Washington uses modular science principles for interoperable and
extensible open research practices.
- Why don't I turn this down a little bit? There's a little bit of an echo.
Or is it this?
Turn that off. Better? Better. Yes.
So my name is Matt Akamatsu. I'm an assistant professor of biology at the University of Washington.
And today I'll show how discourse graphs help our labs to enable modular and interoperable research practices.
So I started my lab at the University of Washington about three and a half years ago.
Here we are on the right. And I've also co-founded the discourse graphs project, which is an open science
and collaboration, tooling, software development platform, and organization.
And through these two organizations, we design, build, and test new ways to do scientific research
that enables better, faster research collaboration and sharing of our research outputs in real time.
These projects are motivated by many of the systemic barriers to research collaboration that Rowan just described
that have been exacerbated now that generative AI is a regular participant in the scientific research
and publishing process. That is best exemplified by this scandal that broke out sometime last year
in the International Conference on Learning Representations, where it became apparent that the majority of,
or many of the submissions as well as reviews for this conference, for these conference papers on
machine learning, were entirely generated by low-quality AI that didn't make any sense.
And so that caused this researcher to say we're entering an era in which distinguishing real science
science from low-quality AI will be essential. An AI tool did an analysis of these reviews and submissions
that indicated that over 20% of the submissions were entirely AI-generated and another large percentage
of them were moderately AI-generated. And so it says it seems that 21% of these reviews may be AI.
But that leads to the same type of question, how strongly should we trust this
particular report and this tool? So the caveat, LLM-generated text detection is not perfect.
So this is illustrating that we need some better grounded piece of information, some evidence to
help us to weigh how much we should trust this measurement and by extension how much we should trust
these scientific research submissions that up until now have been the substrate for how you share
your research communication. But that's under tremendous strain because of large language models.
So we wonder, in this day and age, what is the appropriate source code for scientific communication
in the age of AI? And I pose this question tongue-in-cheek because, as many of you know,
the role of source code itself has changed quite a bit over the past year in programming because it now can be
freely generated by AI and regenerated from specific instructions. So we expect the same transformation
may occur for scientific research communication, where up until now the state-of-the-art has been
this long-form narrative PDF that's dozens of pages long, compiles five years worth of work,
and several dozen results into this single document with a linear authorship list. Likely, we'll need some
other substrate for communicating our research that is smaller than this long-form journal PDF,
but larger than the individual raw data or text. And our principles of operation, as Rowan introduced,
are that we'd like this system to be reusable so that we can build upon different elements of the research
process and synthesize them into living theories of knowledge about our favorite corner of the natural
world. We need each element to have clear provenance and attribution, and we'd love for it to center human
decision-making in an era when the AI scientists are getting increasingly powerful. Absent a deliberate
design for collaboration, we might find ourselves out of a job for the fun parts of doing science.
Therefore, our goal is to decouple research into modular elements that each can be reused, cited,
and synthesized. Those atomic elements are questions, claims, and evidence, which we connect into a graph.
This data model comes from the science philosopher Stephen Tullman, who argues that science is less
a series of monolithic truths and more a series of claims that are supported by more or less evidence.
And over time, you gather more evidence, some of the claims go away and new claims arise. And so we'd like
to be able to capture not only the claims, but the evidence that supports them so that we can update
them. And this particular project is the product of a five and a half year collaboration between myself
and human computer interaction researcher Joelle Chan at the University of Maryland. And together,
we build both the information model and plugins to popular note-taking software so that we can run our labs
on this modular research paradigm and test what works, what genuinely helps the advancement of our lab's
research. So to this end, we've built discourse graphs, which are both a protocol and an app for
interoperable knowledge exchange. Here's the information model. We take our scientific arguments and projects
and decouple them into questions, claims and evidence and connect them into a graph. The question is the unknown
that you'd like to make known with your research project. The claim is the current answer to the research
question, which it wants to be supported by one or more lines of evidence. Each piece of evidence is a specific
observation from your work with an outcome and it's grounded in a single study or experiment or at least
a published research article. The article grounds the evidence. The evidence supports or opposes the claim
and the claim, when incomplete, always motivates a new experiment that you want to do driving the research
research cycle forward. I'll give a historical example. 70 years ago, there were two competing claims for
what is the three-dimensional structure of DNA. Is it a double helix or is it a triple helix? Where are the
negatively charged phosphates in this DNA helix? And there was no evidence to distinguish between these two
competing claims until Rosalind Franklin's famous x-ray diffraction experiment showed this very characteristic
diffraction pattern that gave us some information about what the spacing is likely to be between these DNA
bases and that supported the double helix model for the structure of DNA. So in this discourse graphs framing,
Rosalind Franklin could have posted this single result to the group, to the group's personal data server,
I suppose, and said, "This is a result. It's reproducible. I'm going to show you -- I'm going to tell you the
observation. I'm going to tell you the -- I'm going to give you the associated figure and a little methods context.
Let's figure out what it means together." And in order to strengthen their claim that DNA has a double helix
structure, Watson and Crick would have needed to cite Franklin's piece of evidence. Otherwise, it would
remain an untested claim. And what I love about this model is that it creates an automatic attribution
structure that appropriately credits Franklin's contribution to this discovery over the course of just
making a stronger scientific argument. Alternatively, in a world absent evidence, the modelers could instead request
that somebody do this DNA diffraction experiment because it's important for their model. And then
some -- an experimentalist might claim that experiment, carry it out, tell them what the result was.
And then the modelers get some credit for their intellectual contribution to the idea. And the
experimentalists get their credit for doing the work. In both cases, the attribution trail isn't some
after-the-fact accounting. Instead, it's baked into the ongoing research process.
So we got very excited about this model and whether it might support our researchers'
everyday work and also allow for this natural sort of self-organizing system for modular science.
So we've spent the last number of years developing open source plug-ins for some of our favorite
note-taking apps like Obsidian or Rome Research in which I'm presenting in order to make life easier
for the researchers in our labs. These plug-ins help grad students, postdocs, undergraduates to orient
their projects in context with open questions and key hypotheses and keep them on track towards the
question that they decided they wanted to answer. It allows them to inventory super early stage results
as soon as they think they might have an observation and share them with the team every week in our group
meetings and then synthesize those results into scientific arguments and scientific stories in context with
the published literature so that they have rigorous and updatable stories of the state of knowledge for
their research field. And so we've found that running our labs on this information model has enabled
large-scale collaboration, coordination, and sense-making among members of our lab. So here's a couple examples
showing how using discourse graphs as our lab's graph-based shared lab notebook and meetings documentation
and storyboard for our manuscripts and stories has led to a richly interconnected graph of questions,
claims, claims, and evidence that our lab runs on. In this visualization, each line represents a single
reference of one person's question, claim, or evidence by another person in our 10-person lab that grows
over the three and a half years of our lab's creation, which shows how around 3,500 of these cross-author
references have been generated, indicating that we are building off of each other's intellectual work in
real time. This is both evidence from the published literature as well as new evidence from our ongoing
experiments and simulations. And we're even building theories of knowledge where one person's evidence
supports somebody else's hypothesis. Here's another example showing that we're not only sharing knowledge,
but we're also distributing the work, the requested experiments. In this plot we show
the transfer of a requested experiment. Somebody says, "You know, this analysis experiment is a good idea.
Somebody should do it. I don't have time to do it right now, but either me in the future or an undergraduate
will come in, see this dashboard, claim it, and say, "This is how I can contribute to important problems in the lab."
And so this diagram shows how the transfer of experiments that were requested and then claimed,
often by a different person in the lab, and some of which culminate in a new observation that completes
the experiment. I have the result. I have the analysis. Here's the outcome. We can close this issue.
And so this show, and the other thing I love about this transfer is that a number of these posts and
claiming and completion of experiments were done entirely without my knowledge until they were completed,
which as the principal investigator gives me a lot of assurance that researchers are getting the mentorship
that they need from each other, finding important work to do, and having a very clear trail of who did the work
work in a self-organizing fashion. So in this case, something like 30% of these
issues, requests for experiments, were claimed. 40% of them yielded a result, and 15% of those
happened between different lab members.
So with this proof of concept within our own labs, we now seek to bring these tools in a pilot program to
other research labs throughout North America. So we're halfway through a user pilot supported by the
Chan Zuckerberg Initiative and the Navigation Fund, in which we develop these plugins so that they are easy to
use for these research labs and organizations so that they may better track their experiments,
post research updates, and synthesize their work into their ongoing manuscripts, etc.
What we found so far is that these discourse graphs help researchers, for example, synthesize results
into scientific stories in context with the literature. First, in this non-linear format,
akin to the floor of Lego bricks that Rowan just described, here we're looking at an open canvas
where the researcher in Obsidian can share these individual experiments, results, meaning evidence,
and claims in this open graph along with whatever notes he might want to include and connect them into
this non-linear graph. He calls this his murder board or his whodunit pertaining to his research question
that is grounded in the individual evidence from the literature and from his ongoing experimental observations.
And he uses it to plan what is the right experiment to do next and communicate to the rest of his lab
what his findings are. We're making it possible for him to share individual results with collaborators or
wider networks of researchers, but it's also possible to compile these individual results into a figure for
a traditional manuscript if that's what you need to do. So here's an example from our lab where an
undergraduate is compiling the figures for his manuscript starting with the question. Each panel
of the figure corresponds to one piece of evidence. It's an observation that you put there because it's
strengthening your overall claim that you're trying to make in the figure. So using discourse graphs
demystifies the process of compiling or recompiling your scientific narrative from the underlying evidence.
We've found so far that discourse graphs help researchers in the pilot labs and in our own labs
to do the following. It helps them think like a scientist. People report through qualitative research that
we're doing with Joelle's lab that they have found huge improvements in their thinking and doing of science.
And even when they're not using the plug-in, it helps keep them in the frame of mind. They call it the
discourse graphs frame of mind where they remember their goal is to be addressing questions by providing
evidence that supports or opposes their hypotheses. It also helps them feel like a scientist, saying that they
now have the confidence to share a result without having to claim that they have the be-all end-all
interpretation of that result. They say this is the observation. Let's all figure out together what you think it means.
I'm just an undergraduate. And so now they have the confidence to share their results much earlier than they would have
otherwise given that they're sharing it within a trusted environment of their lab or their collaborators.
Here's another researcher saying that now I have a motivation because I feel like I have
permission to use the words "hypothesis" supporting my hypotheses because they're now baked into the way
that I'm sharing my work. So now I can use those words and that makes it much easier and makes me feel like
I'm a scientist. And so it feels like it's true progress where I'm not just sort of collecting data,
but I'm generating novel observations instead of just where do I start. And very excitingly, we have an early indication
that some researchers feel that documenting their research in discourse graphs better reflects the
true nature of doing science than writing up narratives and making slideshows. So they say converting to
this kind of structure is maybe more aligned with what the actual process is like of answering research
questions that can be complementary to writing up your long-form narratives, but may end up being
more useful and truer to the way that the process of science that people actually want to be carrying out,
particularly in collaborations. I'm also excited about how having this modular graph structure makes it easy
for our research knowledge base to interact with AIs so that they can give us attributable synthesized
information from our lab knowledge base. So here I'm plugging in our lab graph into our favorite AI,
Claude, and gave it some instructions for how it can navigate our graph and asking it to give me an update on
on recent results so that we can have a real-time view of the key results in the lab and a type of real-time
research assessment. So it says here are the recent results from the lab and I'll give you some links,
I'll tell you who did it, I'll tell you how important it is, and we can, you know, vibe code
or any arbitrary visualization or dashboard that we think might be relevant. And this, I think, reflects the
the heterogeneity of algorithms that ATProto enables too, where once you and your community decide
what the relevant metrics or what's the most important thing, then you can show for your
community the highest relevance, in this case individual results connected to the key questions,
motivated by a piece of evidence that I collected that's in my one of my postdoc papers and connected
to an undergraduate's result as well that you can freely navigate through the graph and then also
contribute to. We now can share our research not only in long-form narratives but also in these modular
research elements which we call the evidence bundle. The evidence bundle is a data object that contains
the observation, the key figure, and a little bit of methods context and optionally the the raw data and
the plots that led to the creation of this plot, which get remixed and reused in different contexts.
The evidence bundle was born in my lab notebook in Rome and I use our discourse graph software to inventory it
and update it over time. It then becomes a persistent object in the lab's graph with enough information
for the rest of the lab to understand it, which we can then connect into these mini discourse graphs that
support our ongoing hypotheses. We put this in the repository somewhere and then we can use it to compile
either a traditional narrative or multiple narratives in different venues for different audiences and then we can
also post it on on blue sky. Right now as a prototype this evidence bundle lives in github but there's no
reason it couldn't live on a personal data server and then take advantage of the many apps and tools that many
of you in the audience are building and I would love to do that together. So we are very excited about the
promise for modular research as a way to support people's ongoing science. We think for it to take
hold we need to have the attribution of your modular research contributions be more valuable useful and
accurate than the current method of counting up the authorships in journal articles. So we are working
on that technology and on demonstrating the ability to do modular research attribution. So here you might
have two labs that are working on a similar question. We'd like to make it possible if they each have their
own discourse graphs for each person to be able to submit one result and have it cited by the other lab or
their larger community and one lab can request an experiment. Each result has shared upon metadata in a
schema of the type that Rowan just described so that you can attribute each person's contribution in a way
that initially requires some access control so that they're willing to share their work individually and
then is more widely shareable. We're making this possible in a workshop catalyzing modular interoperable
research attribution and in a workshop in Ireland in June where we're bringing together tool builders and
researchers to make a proof of concept a system by which you can trade individual research elements
and Ronan and I have co-founded a community of organizations called the Kairos Network which is
meant to support different tool building organizations that have shared values that are committed to
interoperability so that modular research can be carried out with the hope that it becomes a
persistent and sustainable source of support for this tool this new tool building ecosystem.
So in summary by decoupling research into its atomic elements questions claims and evidence and connecting
them into a graph we enable trainees experts and AIs alike to make discrete contributions to shared research
collaborations in a way where you can see the direct provenance you can attribute each person's contribution
and you can build upon it in a way that leaves space for each researcher to be recognized and allows them to work
collaboratively with AIs and with each other and preserve the most fun parts of doing research together.
Thank you.
